I first noticed it in the late 1960s when I was a sophomore at a state university. My physics professor, a kind man, would often say, “You’re a woman, so you might not grasp the equations.” Even though I had earned top grades, I felt a knot tighten in my stomach every time a test was announced. That knot was the stereotype threat, a mental fog that made me doubt my own abilities. The phenomenon is documented by Steele (1997) and has been shown to reduce performance among women, minorities, and LGBTQ+ students in STEM. As an older, queer woman with four children, I have seen how this threat can spill over from the classroom to the workplace, discouraging talented individuals from pursuing advanced careers. The question is not whether we should address it—no, we must—but how. The first step is awareness. Teaching students to recognize when they feel the pressure to prove themselves can help them reframe the situation. Mentorship programs, especially those pairing students with role models who share their identity, can mitigate the fear by showing that success is possible. Inclusive classroom practices—such as giving all students equal speaking time, using diverse examples in problem sets, and celebrating non-traditional achievements—transform the learning environment from a “battlefield” to a collaborative lab. On a personal level, I’ve found that talking to friends over coffee or hiking helps me decompress; the same social support network can buffer the anxiety that fuels stereotype threat. Institutions should also conduct regular climate surveys and actively adjust policies when disparities emerge. Finally, research should continue to refine interventions, grounding them in evidence rather than ideology. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat is the subtle, yet powerful, fear that one’s performance will confirm a negative stereotype about a group. I first noticed it in the late 1960s when I was a sophomore at a state university. My physics professor, a kind man, would often say, “You’re a woman, so you might not grasp the equations.” Even though I had earned top grades, I felt a knot tighten in my stomach every time a test was announced. That knot was the stereotype threat, a mental fog that made me doubt my own abilities. The phenomenon is documented by Steele (1997) and has been shown to reduce performance among women, minorities, and LGBTQ+ students in STEM. As an older, queer woman with four children, I have seen how this threat can spill over from the classroom to the workplace, discouraging talented individuals from pursuing advanced careers. The question is not whether we should address it—no, we must—but how. The first step is awareness. Teaching students to recognize when they feel the pressure to prove themselves can help them reframe the situation. Mentorship programs, especially those pairing students with role models who share their identity, can mitigate the fear by showing that success is possible. Inclusive classroom practices—such as giving all students equal speaking time, using diverse examples in problem sets, and celebrating non-traditional achievements—transform the learning environment from a “battlefield” to a collaborative lab. On a personal level, I’ve found that talking to friends over coffee or hiking helps me decompress; the same social support network can buffer the anxiety that fuels stereotype threat. Institutions should also conduct regular climate surveys and actively adjust policies when disparities emerge. Finally, research should continue to refine interventions, grounding them in evidence rather than ideology. In the end, addressing stereotype threat is a moral imperative: it is about equity, about giving every curious mind the chance to shine, and about honoring the fact that my own career—though it began with a trembling heart—was built on the courage to keep learning despite the whispers of doubt.
